failure_modes.description: A port remains in DISCARDING (blocking) state when it is expected to transition to FORWARDING, causing traffic to take a suboptimal path or be black-holed.

resolutions.action: Verify STP topology: 'show spanning-tree' — check root bridge identity
Verify STP topology: 'show spanning-tree' — check root bridge identity
Verify STP topology: 'show spanning-tree' — check root bridge identity
Check for unexpected root bridge: compare bridge priorities
Check for unexpected root bridge: compare bridge priorities
Check for unexpected root bridge: compare bridge priorities
If BPDU guard triggered: resolve rogue BPDU source, then 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' or errdisable recovery
If BPDU guard triggered: resolve rogue BPDU source, then 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' or errdisable recovery
If BPDU guard triggered: resolve rogue BPDU source, then 'shutdown' / 'no shutdown' or errdisable recovery
If root guard: verify intended topology design
If root guard: verify intended topology design
If root guard: verify intended topology design
If loop guard: check for unidirectional link failure
If loop guard: check for unidirectional link failure
If loop guard: check for unidirectional link failure

root_causes.cause: Receiving superior BPDUs — another bridge has lower priority/cost
Root guard blocking port
Loop guard blocking port
BPDU guard triggered (port moved to err-disabled)
Duplicate bridge IDs in the network

symptoms: Port shows BLK or DIS state when it should be forwarding
Traffic not passing through expected path

failure_modes.description: Excessive Topology Change Notifications cause repeated MAC address table flushes, leading to flooding, high CPU, and network instability.

resolutions.action: Identify the port originating TCs: 'show spanning-tree detail | include TC'
Identify the port originating TCs: 'show spanning-tree detail | include TC'
Identify the port originating TCs: 'show spanning-tree detail | include TC'
Configure edge ports as portfast: 'spanning-tree portfast'
Configure edge ports as portfast: 'spanning-tree portfast'
Configure edge ports as portfast: 'spanning-tree portfast'
Fix flapping links
Fix flapping links
Fix flapping links
Consider 'spanning-tree portfast bpdufilter' on known host ports (use with caution)
Consider 'spanning-tree portfast bpdufilter' on known host ports (use with caution)
Consider 'spanning-tree portfast bpdufilter' on known host ports (use with caution)

root_causes.cause: Flapping link causing repeated topology changes
Server NIC teaming misconfigured (sending BPDUs)
Port-channel member bouncing
Edge port not configured as portfast but should be

symptoms: Frequent MAC address table flushes
High CPU from STP processing
'show spanning-tree detail' shows high TC count

failure_modes.description: The root bridge is not the intended switch, causing suboptimal traffic paths across the Layer 2 domain.

resolutions.action: Set root bridge priority explicitly: 'spanning-tree vlan X root primary' or 'spanning-tree vlan X priority 4096'
Set root bridge priority explicitly: 'spanning-tree vlan X root primary' or 'spanning-tree vlan X priority 4096'
Set root bridge priority explicitly: 'spanning-tree vlan X root primary' or 'spanning-tree vlan X priority 4096'
Configure root guard on ports where root bridge should never be learned
Configure root guard on ports where root bridge should never be learned
Configure root guard on ports where root bridge should never be learned
Document and enforce root bridge placement policy
Document and enforce root bridge placement policy
Document and enforce root bridge placement policy
Audit all switches for default bridge priority — any switch at 32768 is a latent risk
Audit all switches for default bridge priority — any switch at 32768 is a latent risk
Audit all switches for default bridge priority — any switch at 32768 is a latent risk

root_causes.cause: Bridge priority not configured — default 32768 on all switches
New switch added with lower MAC address
Root bridge rebooted and another switch took over
Dormant misconfiguration: bridge priorities left at default for months/years, then a new device triggers unexpected election

symptoms: Suboptimal traffic path
Root bridge is an access switch instead of core/distribution
Unexpected root bridge ID in 'show spanning-tree'

failure_modes.description: A physical cable loop or rogue device creates a Layer 2 forwarding loop, causing a broadcast storm that saturates bandwidth and drives CPU to 100%. This is often the highest-impact L2 failure — the entire VLAN or flat L2 domain becomes unusable within seconds.

resolutions.action: IMMEDIATE: Identify the loop ports from MAC move notifications — 'show mac address-table notifications mac-move'
IMMEDIATE: Identify the loop ports from MAC move notifications — 'show mac address-table notifications mac-move'
IMMEDIATE: Identify the loop ports from MAC move notifications — 'show mac address-table notifications mac-move'
IMMEDIATE: Shut the offending port(s) — 'interface <intf>' then 'shutdown'. Start with the port most likely to be the rogue connection (access port, not uplink)
IMMEDIATE: Shut the offending port(s) — 'interface <intf>' then 'shutdown'. Start with the port most likely to be the rogue connection (access port, not uplink)
IMMEDIATE: Shut the offending port(s) — 'interface <intf>' then 'shutdown'. Start with the port most likely to be the rogue connection (access port, not uplink)
IMMEDIATE: Verify CPU drops and traffic resumes — 'show processes cpu sort' should show CPU recovering
IMMEDIATE: Verify CPU drops and traffic resumes — 'show processes cpu sort' should show CPU recovering
IMMEDIATE: Verify CPU drops and traffic resumes — 'show processes cpu sort' should show CPU recovering
PREVENTIVE: Enable BPDU Guard on all access/edge ports — 'spanning-tree port type edge' + 'spanning-tree bpduguard enable' (per-interface) or 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' (global)
PREVENTIVE: Enable BPDU Guard on all access/edge ports — 'spanning-tree port type edge' + 'spanning-tree bpduguard enable' (per-interface) or 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' (global)
PREVENTIVE: Enable BPDU Guard on all access/edge ports — 'spanning-tree port type edge' + 'spanning-tree bpduguard enable' (per-interface) or 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' (global)
PREVENTIVE: Enable storm control on access ports — 'storm-control broadcast level 10' limits broadcast to 10% of link capacity
PREVENTIVE: Enable storm control on access ports — 'storm-control broadcast level 10' limits broadcast to 10% of link capacity
PREVENTIVE: Enable storm control on access ports — 'storm-control broadcast level 10' limits broadcast to 10% of link capacity
PREVENTIVE: Enable errdisable recovery for bpduguard — 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' with 'errdisable recovery interval 300'
PREVENTIVE: Enable errdisable recovery for bpduguard — 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' with 'errdisable recovery interval 300'
PREVENTIVE: Enable errdisable recovery for bpduguard — 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' with 'errdisable recovery interval 300'
DESIGN: Reduce flat L2 domain size — use routed access or L3 boundaries to limit blast radius
DESIGN: Reduce flat L2 domain size — use routed access or L3 boundaries to limit blast radius
DESIGN: Reduce flat L2 domain size — use routed access or L3 boundaries to limit blast radius

root_causes.cause: Physical cable loop — someone patched a cable between two access ports on the same switch or VLAN
Unmanaged switch or hub connected to two ports without STP awareness
BPDU Guard not enabled on access ports — STP cannot detect the loop because edge ports skip STP by design
Storm control not configured — no circuit breaker to limit broadcast/multicast flood rate
Flat L2 domain too large — blast radius is the entire VLAN instead of a contained segment
Server NIC teaming misconfigured — bridging between two NICs connected to different switch ports

symptoms: Switch CPU at or near 100%
All or most VLANs unreachable — intermittent or zero ping replies
MAC move notifications flooding syslog ('MAC_MOVE_NOTIFICATION')
MAC addresses rapidly flapping between two or more ports in 'show mac address-table notifications mac-move'
Interface counters show massive input/output rates on loop ports
Users report network completely down or extremely slow

failure_modes.description: A fiber strand fails in one direction (TX works but RX does not, or vice versa) due to a bad SFP, dirty fiber, or faulty patch panel. The switch on one side thinks the link is up but the other side does not receive BPDUs. The port that should be blocking transitions to forwarding because it no longer sees the root bridge, creating a loop. Without UDLD aggressive mode, this failure is undetectable by STP alone.

resolutions.action: IMMEDIATE: If loop detected, shut the affected port
IMMEDIATE: If loop detected, shut the affected port
IMMEDIATE: If loop detected, shut the affected port
DIAGNOSE: Check optics: 'show interface transceiver' — compare TX/RX power on both ends. RX power near -30 dBm or 'N/A' indicates receive failure
DIAGNOSE: Check optics: 'show interface transceiver' — compare TX/RX power on both ends. RX power near -30 dBm or 'N/A' indicates receive failure
DIAGNOSE: Check optics: 'show interface transceiver' — compare TX/RX power on both ends. RX power near -30 dBm or 'N/A' indicates receive failure
DIAGNOSE: Check interface counters: 'show interface' — one side will show zero input packets while output is normal
DIAGNOSE: Check interface counters: 'show interface' — one side will show zero input packets while output is normal
DIAGNOSE: Check interface counters: 'show interface' — one side will show zero input packets while output is normal
FIX: Replace SFP or clean/replace fiber connectors
FIX: Replace SFP or clean/replace fiber connectors
FIX: Replace SFP or clean/replace fiber connectors
PREVENTIVE: Enable UDLD aggressive mode globally: 'udld aggressive' (IOS-XE) or 'feature udld' + 'udld aggressive' (NX-OS)
PREVENTIVE: Enable UDLD aggressive mode globally: 'udld aggressive' (IOS-XE) or 'feature udld' + 'udld aggressive' (NX-OS)
PREVENTIVE: Enable UDLD aggressive mode globally: 'udld aggressive' (IOS-XE) or 'feature udld' + 'udld aggressive' (NX-OS)
PREVENTIVE: Enable Loop Guard on all non-designated (root/alternate) ports: 'spanning-tree loopguard default'
PREVENTIVE: Enable Loop Guard on all non-designated (root/alternate) ports: 'spanning-tree loopguard default'
PREVENTIVE: Enable Loop Guard on all non-designated (root/alternate) ports: 'spanning-tree loopguard default'
PREVENTIVE: Enable errdisable recovery for udld: 'errdisable recovery cause udld'
PREVENTIVE: Enable errdisable recovery for udld: 'errdisable recovery cause udld'
PREVENTIVE: Enable errdisable recovery for udld: 'errdisable recovery cause udld'

root_causes.cause: Bad SFP/transceiver — TX laser working but RX photodiode failed
Dirty or damaged fiber connector — one strand degraded below receive threshold
Faulty fiber patch panel with one strand disconnected
UDLD not enabled or not in aggressive mode — without UDLD aggressive, the switch cannot detect one-way links
Loop Guard not enabled on non-designated ports

symptoms: Broadcast storm on specific VLANs despite STP appearing healthy
One switch shows the link as up, the other shows it as down or never receives BPDUs
STP port that should be blocking transitions to forwarding (no BPDUs received = max-age expiry = port unblocks)
Interface error counters: CRC errors, input errors, or zero input packets on one side
Loop Guard may trigger 'inconsistent' state if enabled — this is the detection working correctly

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows UDLD neighbor detection status per interface
verify_commands.look_for: Look for UDLD neighbor state — bidirectional means healthy, empty/unknown means one-way link detected

verify_commands.look_for: Compare TX power (should be -1 to -8 dBm typical) and RX power (should be within vendor spec) on both ends

failure_modes.description: The STP root bridge for a VLAN is on one switch but the HSRP/VRRP active gateway for that VLAN is on a different switch. Traffic from hosts hits the FHRP active gateway, which must then forward across the inter-switch link to reach the STP root — wasting bandwidth and adding latency. This is a design issue that causes chronic suboptimal forwarding.

resolutions.action: ALIGN: For each VLAN, ensure STP root primary and HSRP active are on the same switch
ALIGN: For each VLAN, ensure STP root primary and HSRP active are on the same switch
ALIGN: For each VLAN, ensure STP root primary and HSRP active are on the same switch
DISTRIBUTE: For load distribution, make Switch A root + HSRP active for odd VLANs, Switch B for even VLANs
DISTRIBUTE: For load distribution, make Switch A root + HSRP active for odd VLANs, Switch B for even VLANs
DISTRIBUTE: For load distribution, make Switch A root + HSRP active for odd VLANs, Switch B for even VLANs
CONFIG: Set STP priority: 'spanning-tree vlan <X> priority 4096' on the intended root
CONFIG: Set STP priority: 'spanning-tree vlan <X> priority 4096' on the intended root
CONFIG: Set STP priority: 'spanning-tree vlan <X> priority 4096' on the intended root
CONFIG: Set HSRP priority: 'standby <group> priority 110' + 'standby <group> preempt' on the same switch
CONFIG: Set HSRP priority: 'standby <group> priority 110' + 'standby <group> preempt' on the same switch
CONFIG: Set HSRP priority: 'standby <group> priority 110' + 'standby <group> preempt' on the same switch
VERIFY: After changes, check alignment: 'show spanning-tree root' and 'show standby brief' should point to the same switch per VLAN
VERIFY: After changes, check alignment: 'show spanning-tree root' and 'show standby brief' should point to the same switch per VLAN
VERIFY: After changes, check alignment: 'show spanning-tree root' and 'show standby brief' should point to the same switch per VLAN

root_causes.cause: STP priority and HSRP priority not aligned — different switches configured as primary for each
After a failover, HSRP preempted back but STP root did not (STP DR election is non-preemptive in some scenarios)
Default priorities used for both STP and HSRP — election by MAC/Router-ID gives different winners
Multiple VLANs all using the same root/FHRP active — no per-VLAN load distribution

failure_modes.scenario: STP root bridge / FHRP gateway misalignment
symptoms: Traffic between VLANs takes an unnecessarily long path
High utilization on inter-switch links (ISL/peer-link) that should be lightly loaded
Traceroute shows extra hop through the non-root switch
'show spanning-tree root' shows root on Switch A but 'show standby brief' shows HSRP active on Switch B for the same VLAN

verify_commands.look_for: Root bridge ID and root port per VLAN — identify which switch is root for each VLAN

verify_commands.look_for: HSRP active router per group/VLAN — compare with STP root placement

verify_commands.look_for: HSRP active router per group/VLAN — compare with STP root placement

verify_commands.confirms_if: Shows which switch is HSRP active for each VLAN
verify_commands.look_for: HSRP active router per group/VLAN — compare with STP root placement

failure_modes.description: Network experiences 30-50 second forwarding outages during topology changes because legacy 802.1D STP is in use (listening 15s -> learning 15s -> forwarding), or because RSTP has fallen back to legacy timers due to detecting 802.1D BPDUs from an older switch. End devices connected to non-portfast ports also experience 30-second delays on link-up.

resolutions.action: Upgrade STP mode to rapid-pvst: 'spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst' on ALL switches in the L2 domain
Upgrade STP mode to rapid-pvst: 'spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst' on ALL switches in the L2 domain
Upgrade STP mode to rapid-pvst: 'spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst' on ALL switches in the L2 domain
Identify legacy switches: 'show spanning-tree detail' — look for ports operating in STP-compatible mode
Identify legacy switches: 'show spanning-tree detail' — look for ports operating in STP-compatible mode
Identify legacy switches: 'show spanning-tree detail' — look for ports operating in STP-compatible mode
Configure all access ports as edge: 'spanning-tree port type edge' (NX-OS) or 'spanning-tree portfast' (IOS-XE)
Configure all access ports as edge: 'spanning-tree port type edge' (NX-OS) or 'spanning-tree portfast' (IOS-XE)
Configure all access ports as edge: 'spanning-tree port type edge' (NX-OS) or 'spanning-tree portfast' (IOS-XE)
Replace or upgrade any switch that cannot run RSTP
Replace or upgrade any switch that cannot run RSTP
Replace or upgrade any switch that cannot run RSTP

root_causes.cause: STP mode set to legacy PVST (802.1D) instead of Rapid-PVST (802.1w)
One or more switches in the L2 domain running legacy 802.1D, causing RSTP to fall back to STP timers on those segments
Access ports not configured as edge/portfast — end devices wait through full STP convergence on link-up
Forward-delay timer set to high value

failure_modes.scenario: Slow STP convergence (legacy 802.1D or mixed mode)
symptoms: 30-50 second connectivity loss during any topology change
VoIP phones, printers, PCs report 'network unreachable' for ~30s after cable plug-in
'show spanning-tree' shows port in 'LIS' (listening) or 'LRN' (learning) state for extended time
STP mode shows 'pvst' instead of 'rapid-pvst'

verify_commands.confirms_if: Confirms whether the switch is running RSTP or legacy STP
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'Switch is in rapid-pvst mode' — if it shows 'pvst' instead of 'rapid-pvst', legacy STP is in use

verify_commands.confirms_if: Confirms whether the switch is running RSTP or legacy STP
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'Switch is in rapid-pvst mode' — if it shows 'pvst' instead of 'rapid-pvst', legacy STP is in use

verify_commands.confirms_if: Confirms whether the switch is running RSTP or legacy STP
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'Switch is in rapid-pvst mode' — if it shows 'pvst' instead of 'rapid-pvst', legacy STP is in use

verify_commands.confirms_if: Identifies which ports/segments have fallen back to legacy STP timers
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'RSTP' in port info sections — ports showing 'STP' or 'STP_COMPAT' are running in legacy mode

verify_commands.confirms_if: Identifies which ports/segments have fallen back to legacy STP timers
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'RSTP' in port info sections — ports showing 'STP' or 'STP_COMPAT' are running in legacy mode

verify_commands.confirms_if: Identifies which ports/segments have fallen back to legacy STP timers
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'RSTP' in port info sections — ports showing 'STP' or 'STP_COMPAT' are running in legacy mode

failure_modes.description: Access ports configured as edge/portfast but without BPDU Guard create a latent loop risk. These ports bypass STP (immediate forwarding) by design. If someone plugs a switch, hub, or looped cable into an unprotected edge port, a broadcast storm occurs with no STP safety net. This is the most common latent error in L2 networks — it causes no symptoms until the day someone creates a loop, then the entire VLAN goes down.

resolutions.action: AUDIT: Check current BPDU Guard coverage — 'show spanning-tree summary' should show BPDU Guard enabled on all edge ports
AUDIT: Check current BPDU Guard coverage — 'show spanning-tree summary' should show BPDU Guard enabled on all edge ports
AUDIT: Check current BPDU Guard coverage — 'show spanning-tree summary' should show BPDU Guard enabled on all edge ports
FIX: Enable global BPDU Guard default — 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' (NX-OS) or 'spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default' (IOS-XE)
FIX: Enable global BPDU Guard default — 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' (NX-OS) or 'spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default' (IOS-XE)
FIX: Enable global BPDU Guard default — 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' (NX-OS) or 'spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default' (IOS-XE)
FIX: Enable storm control on all access ports — 'storm-control broadcast level 10' as a secondary safety layer
FIX: Enable storm control on all access ports — 'storm-control broadcast level 10' as a secondary safety layer
FIX: Enable storm control on all access ports — 'storm-control broadcast level 10' as a secondary safety layer
FIX: Configure errdisable recovery — 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' with 300-second interval so ports auto-recover after the offending device is removed
FIX: Configure errdisable recovery — 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' with 300-second interval so ports auto-recover after the offending device is removed
FIX: Configure errdisable recovery — 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' with 300-second interval so ports auto-recover after the offending device is removed

root_causes.cause: Edge/portfast configured without corresponding BPDU Guard — common in networks that grew organically
Global BPDU Guard not enabled — requires explicit 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' on NX-OS or 'spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default' on IOS-XE
Storm control not configured as a secondary safety layer
No physical access control to wiring closets — unauthorized cable changes go undetected
Flat L2 domain with no segmentation — blast radius of any loop is the entire network

failure_modes.scenario: Missing BPDU Guard on access ports (latent risk)
symptoms: No symptoms until a loop is created — this is a dormant misconfiguration
When triggered: broadcast storm, 100% CPU, all VLANs down (see 'Broadcast storm / L2 loop' failure mode)
Audit indicator: 'show spanning-tree summary' shows edge ports with BPDU Guard disabled

verify_commands.look_for: Check 'BPDU Guard' line — should show 'enabled' if global default is on. Check edge port count versus total port count

messages.description: Bridge Protocol Data Unit — carries STP topology information
key_fields: Flags (TC, TCA, Proposal, Agreement, Port Role)
messages.name: BPDU (Configuration)
messages.when_sent: Every hello-time by root bridge; relayed by designated ports

messages.description: Topology Change Notification (legacy STP; RSTP uses TC flag in BPDU)
messages.name: TCN BPDU
messages.when_sent: When a port transitions to forwarding (non-edge)

purpose: Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol — provides loop-free Layer 2 topology with fast convergence

states.description: Not forwarding, learning, or transmitting user frames. Replaces the legacy BLOCKING and LISTENING states. Normal transient state during topology convergence, but a failure indicator when a port is stuck here unexpectedly.
states.name: DISCARDING

states.description: Learning MAC addresses from received frames but not yet forwarding user traffic. Transient state between DISCARDING and FORWARDING.
states.name: LEARNING

states.description: Fully operational — forwarding user traffic and learning MAC addresses. Steady-state for Root Ports, Designated Ports, and Edge Ports.
states.name: FORWARDING

states.description: Port is administratively disabled (shutdown). Does not participate in STP at all.
states.name: DISABLED

states.description: Port is unable to participate in STP due to an inconsistency (e.g., BPDU Guard, Root Guard, or Loop Guard violation). Requires manual intervention.
states.name: BROKEN

timers.description: Interval between BPDU transmissions by root bridge
timers.name: hello-time
timers.tuning_advice: Rarely changed; affects all switches in the STP domain

timers.description: Time spent in LEARNING before FORWARDING (legacy STP); RSTP uses proposal/agreement for rapid transition
timers.name: forward-delay
timers.tuning_advice: RSTP bypasses this timer on point-to-point links; mainly relevant for shared/edge ports

timers.description: Maximum age of BPDU information before it is discarded
timers.name: max-age
timers.on_expiry: Stored BPDU info expires; port re-evaluates its role
timers.tuning_advice: Lower values detect root failure faster but risk instability

timers.description: Topology Change timer — accelerated MAC aging after TC event
timers.name: tc-while
timers.on_expiry: Stop sending TC flag in BPDUs

transitions.trigger: Selected as root or designated port; proposal/agreement mechanism or forward-delay timer expires
Forward-delay timer expires or rapid transition via proposal/agreement on point-to-point link

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Possible TCN storm or unstable root bridge
transitions.trigger: Topology change — port loses its role (e.g., better BPDU received)
Port role changed to alternate/backup after receiving superior BPDU
Inconsistency resolved (e.g., BPDU guard re-enabled correctly)

config_examples:
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default
errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery interval 300

storm-control broadcast level 10.00
storm-control action shutdown

udld aggressive
errdisable recovery cause udld
errdisable recovery interval 300

notes.note: Default STP mode is rapid-pvst since IOS-XE 16.x on Catalyst 9000. Older platforms may default to pvst.

CRITICAL: Enable BPDU Guard globally on IOS-XE with 'spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default'. All portfast-enabled ports will err-disable if a BPDU is received. Configure 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' with appropriate interval. IOS-XE uses 'spanning-tree portfast' terminology (not 'port type edge').

Storm control on IOS-XE: 'storm-control broadcast level 10.00' per interface. Supports 'storm-control action shutdown' to err-disable the port (recommended) or 'storm-control action trap' for syslog-only. 'show storm-control broadcast' shows per-interface status and filter state.

UDLD on IOS-XE: 'udld enable' for normal mode, 'udld aggressive' for aggressive mode (recommended). Normal mode only alerts; aggressive mode err-disables the port. Enable globally or per-interface. Pair with 'errdisable recovery cause udld' for auto-recovery.
IOS-XR does not run STP — it is a router OS. STP is handled by connected L2 switches.

config_examples:
spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default
errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery interval 300

storm-control broadcast level 10.00
storm-control multicast level 10.00
storm-control action shutdown

notes.note: Default STP mode is rapid-pvst. Configure with 'spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst'. Bridge Assurance enabled by default on network-to-network ports.
NX-OS supports STP extensions: BPDU Guard, BPDU Filter, Root Guard, Loop Guard, Bridge Assurance. 'spanning-tree port type' (network/edge/normal) replaces portfast terminology.

CRITICAL: Every access port should have 'spanning-tree port type edge' AND BPDU Guard enabled. Use 'spanning-tree port type edge bpduguard default' for global default. Uplinks should be 'spanning-tree port type network' which enables Bridge Assurance (detects unidirectional links). Without BPDU Guard, edge ports bypass STP and create a latent loop risk.

Storm control on NX-OS: 'storm-control broadcast level 10.00' sets broadcast threshold to 10% of interface bandwidth. Also supports 'storm-control multicast level' and 'storm-control unicast level'. When triggered, storm control drops excess traffic and generates a syslog message. Use 'show storm-control' to verify per-interface status.

TRAP: MAC move notifications on NX-OS require explicit enable: 'mac address-table notification mac-move'. Without this, L2 loops may not generate syslog alerts. 'show mac address-table notifications mac-move' shows recently moved MACs and the ports involved — this is the fastest way to identify loop endpoints during a broadcast storm.

UDLD on NX-OS requires 'feature udld'. Enable aggressive mode globally with 'udld aggressive'. UDLD detects unidirectional fiber links that STP alone cannot. Without UDLD aggressive, a one-way fiber failure causes a silent loop. Bridge Assurance (enabled by default on 'network' ports) provides similar protection for switch-to-switch links.

Loop Guard on NX-OS: 'spanning-tree loopguard default' enables globally. Loop Guard puts a port into loop-inconsistent state if it stops receiving BPDUs on a root or alternate port. This catches unidirectional link failures that RSTP alone misses. Do NOT enable on edge/portfast ports — it conflicts with BPDU Guard.
